text: The surgeon performs a lateral to medial dissection during a high anterior resection, dividing the lateral attachments of the sigmoid colon. The sigmoid and descending colon are visibly mobilized laterally.

The surgeon continues dissection into the descending colon's peritoneal attachments, using medial retraction to maintain tension and facilitate the process. The lateral dissection progresses towards the splenic flexure, revealing the sidewall anatomy of the colon.

The surgeon retracts the colon laterally to facilitate medial dissection of the vessels. The peritoneum is divided up to the site of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), revealing lateral anatomical structures. Further dissection is performed around the pedicle, which is retracted to confirm the position of the ureter. A stapler is inserted around the pedicle, and the pedicle is divided.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the abdominal side wall, connecting the medial and lateral planes. The medial peritoneal attachments are further divided to increase length. Dissection proceeds in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, releasing bilateral peritoneal attachments of the upper and mid rectum.

The surgeon skeletonizes the rectal tube to facilitate division, using a laparoscopic stapler for intracorporeal division of the rectum. The resected bowel is temporarily removed from the abdomen, where the anvil is placed and then returned to the abdominal cavity. The stapling spike is deployed, connecting the anvil, and the orientation is verified before closing the stapling device. The anastomosis is checked using an air leak test.
